svg.text: Three-point log interpolation opens a compact amplitude lane

text.tspan: The earlier audit asked whether flat-top hides a coarse sampled-peak trap. This follow-up asks the next practical
question: if you allow one tiny local estimator around the peak, does the amplitude story change? For length 129, the
Raw sampled peaks leave a real quarter-dB hole here. A log
parabola collapses it into a few thousandths of a dB.
This is the sharper compact option. The log fit nearly erases
the old amplitude gap while ENBW stays far below flat-top.
Flat-top already wins with no extra work. Interpolation is not
its advantage; the raw sampled peak was honest from the start.
At the coarsest probe in this audit, the amplitude lane splits
in two. Flat-top still wins the no-extra-processing read. But a
three-point log parabola gives Blackman and especially Blackman-
At 256 points, Blackman drops from 0.273 dB to 0.0050 dB and Blackman-
Harris drops from 0.206 dB to 0.0019 dB with a log parabola. Flat-top was
already inside 0.0023 dB raw, while its own log fit rises to 0.0134 dB. So
flat-top keeps the zero-extra-work lane, but it no longer owns amplitude
honesty outright once interpolation is allowed.
